conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children - Alex, who is 11, and Blake, who is 15. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children - Alex (11) and Blake (15). 

Let me ask you a few questions to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both of them.

First, are you responsible for Alex and Blake? By this I mean, do they normally live with you, or do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep (currently at least £25.60 a week for the eldest and £16.95 per week for the younger child)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't live with me. I do send some money to help out with their costs, but it's not that much - I contribute about £10 a week for Alex and nothing currently for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So the children don't live with you, and you contribute £10 per week for Alex and nothing for Blake - which is less than the Child Benefit amounts.

Can you tell me who the children currently live with? Is it their other parent or another person?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They live with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. So Alex and Blake live with their other parent.

Is the other parent currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, their other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. I'm not sure about Alex though.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Are both Alex and Blake under 20 years old and in full-time non-advanced education or approved training? (I know Alex is 11, so this mainly applies to Blake who is 15.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is still in primary school, so yes for him. Blake is in secondary school doing his GCSEs, so yes, he's in full-time education too.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Both children are in full-time education, which meets the age requirements for Child Benefit.

Let me ask one final question: Are Alex and Blake both living in the UK, or are any of them living abroad?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're both living in the UK with their other parent. I'm the one who doesn't live in the UK - I live abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me read the official guidance to make a proper assessment of your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. Based on the information you've provided and the official guidance, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, I'm afraid **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake**. Here's why:

**For Alex (age 11):**
- While Alex meets the age requirement, you don't meet the "responsibility" test for Child Benefit eligibility
- To qualify when a child doesn't live with you, you must contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit (which is £25.60 per week for the eldest child or £16.95 per week for other children)
- You currently contribute £10 per week, which falls below this threshold
- Additionally, you live abroad, which affects your ability to claim UK Child Benefit

**For Blake (age 15):**
- Blake also meets the age requirement (in full-time education)
- However, their other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child
- You don't contribute anything towards Blake's upkeep, so you don't meet the responsibility test
- You also live abroad, which affects your eligibility

If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if you return to live in the UK, or if you increase your contributions to at least the Child Benefit amount), your eligibility could be different. The children's other parent who currently lives with them in the UK is the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit in this situation.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Someone who lives with the child is already claiming

Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 11 years old and in primary school, which meets the age requirement (under 16). However, to be eligible for Child Benefit, you must either: (1) live with the child, or (2) contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. You contribute £10 per week for Alex, which is less than the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for the eldest child or £16.95 per week for other children. Alex lives with their other parent in the UK, and since you don't meet the responsibility test (not living with them and not contributing enough), you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Additionally, you live abroad which may affect your eligibility to claim UK Child Benefit.

Blake is 15 years old and in secondary school doing GCSEs, which meets the age requirement (under 16, or under 20 if in approved education). However, you do not contribute anything financially towards Blake's upkeep, and Blake does not live with you. Blake lives with their other parent in the UK, who is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and since the other parent is already claiming for Blake and you don't meet the responsibility test (not living with Blake and not contributing at least the Child Benefit amount), you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Additionally, you live abroad which may affect your eligibility to claim UK Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children, Alex (11) and Blake (15), both in full-time education in the UK. However, the claimant lives abroad and the children live with their other parent in the UK. The claimant contributes £10 per week for Alex (below the Child Benefit threshold of at least £16.95-£25.60 per week) and nothing for Blake. The other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Since the claimant doesn't live with either child and doesn't contribute enough to meet the responsibility test, and only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake.

However, to be eligible for Child Benefit, you must either: (1) live with the child, or (2) contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. You contribute £10 per week for Alex, which is less than the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week for the eldest child or £16.95 per week for other children. Alex lives with their other parent in the UK, and since you don't meet the responsibility test (not living with them and not contributing enough), you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Additionally, you live abroad which may affect your eligibility to claim UK Child Benefit.

Blake is 15 years old and in secondary school doing GCSEs, which meets the age requirement (under 16, or under 20 if in approved education). However, you do not contribute anything financially towards Blake's upkeep, and Blake does not live with you. Blake lives with their other parent in the UK, who is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and since the other parent is already claiming for Blake and you don't meet the responsibility test (not living with Blake and not contributing at least the Child Benefit amount), you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Additionally, you live abroad which may affect your eligibility to claim UK Child Benefit.

However, the claimant lives abroad and the children live with their other parent in the UK. The claimant contributes £10 per week for Alex (below the Child Benefit threshold of at least £16.95-£25.60 per week) and nothing for Blake. The other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Since the claimant doesn't live with either child and doesn't contribute enough to meet the responsibility test, and only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake.
